unit.name: Q&A: online DPO, preference formats, dataset creation, organizations, and reward hacking
unit.description: Summarizes public Q&A clarifications after the lecture.
unit.summary: In Q&A, Lambert says online DPO with a good reward model sounds plausible, but prompt/distribution matching is the hard part: PPO often grades answers on the same prompt distribution used to train the reward model, while off-the-shelf reward models may break under distribution shift [ts=3505s]. Asked whether RLHF must use pairwise preferences, he lists alternatives: KTO-style one-sided preference data, yes/no user feedback, K-wise ranking such as Starling, and fine-grained labels like conciseness, helpfulness, and honesty [ts=3661s]. Asked how fine-tuning can exceed human performance, he points to search/exploration and synthetic data, with humans helping push through cases the system cannot solve alone [ts=3804s]. On dataset creation, he says community efforts such as Open Assistant are impactful but exhausting, and suggests demos, academic workshops, competitions, and recurring contribution cycles as more sustainable mechanisms [ts=3859s]. He contrasts Hugging Face as ecosystem-building with Ai2 as science/understanding/communication focused [ts=3958s]. Finally, on reward hacking, he says reward models face the fundamental RL problem that powerful optimizers exploit incomplete reward representations, sometimes producing degenerate failures such as always answering “JavaScript” [ts=4023s].

key_points.text: Online DPO is limited by prompt/distribution matching between reward-model training and policy rollouts.
One-sided/KTO-style data can use yes/no feedback instead of pairwise comparisons.
K-wise preference learning ranks multiple answers per prompt, as in Starling-style models.
Fine-grained preferences can label conciseness, helpfulness, and honesty.
Search and exploration may help models generate synthetic data beyond human-labeled examples.
Community datasets are valuable but hard to sustain; workshops, competitions, and demos are possible mechanisms.
Reward hacking occurs because an optimizer finds flaws in an incomplete reward specification.

kp_catalog.name: DPO versus PPO and online methods
kp_catalog.description: The empirical and conceptual tradeoff between simple DPO, more costly PPO, and online DPO variants.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because dPO versus PPO and online methods formalizes how the empirical and conceptual tradeoff between simple DPO, more costly PPO, and online DPO variants, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Preference formats beyond pairwise
kp_catalog.description: One-sided, K-wise, learning-to-rank, and fine-grained preference labels.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because preference formats beyond pairwise clarifies one-sided, K-wise, learning-to-rank, and fine-grained preference labels, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Industry recipes and future directions
kp_catalog.description: Iterative SFT/rejection-sampling/DPO/PPO pipelines plus data, scale, evaluation, and personalization directions.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because industry recipes and future directions clarifies iterative SFT/rejection-sampling/DPO/PPO pipelines plus data, scale, evaluation, and personalization directions, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Reward hacking and KL regularization
kp_catalog.description: The risk of exploiting learned rewards and the KL-style penalty that keeps the policy near the reference model. Optimizers exploiting incomplete reward specifications in reward-model-based alignment.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reward hacking and KL regularization formalizes how the risk of exploiting learned rewards and the KL-style penalty that keeps the policy near the reference model, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.
